video_title: Why Are Bitcoin & AI Stocks CRASHING?!
gist: Macro hedge fund manager Jordy Visser argues the AI selloff is a midcycle slowdown, not the end of the trade, and that memory (Micron) is now a strategic asset in a supply shortage running until 2028. He frames Gemini/Google as the losing #3 in AI, sees Anthropic as potentially undervalued versus SpaceX, and calls the gold/silver/Bitcoin decline a 'debasement capitulation' rather than a structural break.

themes.theme: AI selloff: midcycle slowdown, not a top

insights.claim: Jordy Visser argues the AI trade is in the second or third inning of a long buildout and the recent selloff is a 'midcycle slowdown' rather than the end of the trade, with strength expected to continue in the second half of the year.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Frames the drawdown as a buyable consolidation rather than a regime change for AI-exposed equities.
insights.quote: we are in still the second or third inning of what will be a long buildout

insights.claim: The fear catalyst was SK Hynix (SKHX) trading effectively limit down overnight in the Korean market, which triggered widespread 'this is the crash' commentary on X and podcasts before Micron's earnings changed the tone.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Identifies Korean memory names as the leading-edge sentiment indicator for the AI/memory complex.

insights.claim: Visser dates the inflection in AI capability to October/November of last year when Opus 4.5 came out, arguing the world meaningfully changed then even if most users didn't feel a 'ChatGPT moment'.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Anchors the bull case to a recent capability step-change rather than to price momentum.
insights.quote: The world changed in October of last year or November of last year when Opus 4.5 came out

insights.claim: Visser notes the S&P 500 was up less than 8% in the first half of the year and US hyperscalers had record monthly falls (Microsoft's worst month since 2008), while the Nikkei was up ~45-48%, the Kospi over 100%, and Taiwan over 50%, because the AI 'receivers' carry higher index weight in Asia than the hyperscalers do in the US.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: A 'benchmark arbitrage' case for favoring Asian AI-supplier indices over US hyperscaler-heavy benchmarks.
insights.quote: Microsoft is having its worst month since 2008

insights.claim: Micron reported more revenue in its latest quarter than Nvidia did when Nvidia was a $4 trillion company, yet Micron is not a $4 trillion company; Visser says it would be very surprising if Micron does not reach a $2 trillion market cap over the next year and that Micron is in no way a short.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: A specific 12-month upside target on a memory leader despite the selloff, framed against an Nvidia comparison.
insights.quote: it will be very surprising if they don't get up to two trillion over the course of the next year

insights.claim: Visser argues AI agents created an overnight step-change in memory demand because agents must reason over the past, analogizing it to 4 billion extra people appearing and demanding food, with humanoids and autonomous vehicles needing even more over the next 5-10 years.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Explains the structural, demand-driven nature of the memory shortage that supports prices for years.
insights.quote: The amount of memory that is needed because of AI agents which need to think about the past and this and go through it and connect changed overnight

insights.claim: Micron stated on its earnings call that memory supply and demand will be out of balance until 2028 and that it has long-term commitments from many customers, and the CEO called memory a strategic asset.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: A multi-year supply-shortage runway underpins pricing power for memory names.
insights.quote: supply and demand will be out of balance until 2028

insights.claim: Visser warns of a sharp second-derivative slowdown in Micron earnings, noting Micron did more in the first two quarters of this year than roughly the last 10 years combined, so growth decelerating from ~400% to ~50% is a slowdown driven by price increases and capex bottlenecks, not a down trade.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Sets expectations that decelerating growth will pressure sentiment even as fundamentals stay strong.
insights.quote: if you're only growing at 50% from 400%, that's the second derivative kicking in

insights.claim: Visser says he exited Micron too early and rotated into Marvell as a different way to play the memory shortage.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Flags Marvell as an alternative beneficiary of the memory shortage theme.

insights.claim: Visser cites a Scott Bessent speech at the New York economic club framing AI and industrial capacity as a strategic national need, arguing the US over-consumed and must now produce its own supply (including rare earths) rather than depend on global supply chains exposed by last year's tariffs.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Government strategic backing implies a policy floor under domestic AI infrastructure plays.

insights.claim: Visser frames the US government's strategic investment in Intel as a national-security decision to keep one of the largest US foundries running at full throttle alongside Taiwan Semi, because the US cannot lack CPUs and domestic foundry capacity.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Reframes Intel as a strategic-capacity bet backed by government rather than a pure competitive turnaround.

insights.claim: Visser argues governments are in a military-style race for AI dominance, points to DeepSeek's funding round where only the Chinese government received voting rights, and concludes investors should favor companies building AI infrastructure over those monetizing the models.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Tilts the trade toward hardware/infrastructure over model monetizers.
insights.quote: you want to invest in the things that are going to build the AI, maybe not as so much as the people that are going to monetize the models

insights.claim: Visser says Claude and ChatGPT now make up about 90% of his AI usage, up from a roughly 35% Claude / 25% ChatGPT / 20% Google split, with Gemini, Grok, and Perplexity becoming less important to him.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: A power user's usage shift is a leading signal of which model providers are winning enterprise mindshare.
insights.quote: Chachi PT and Claude are effectively 90% of my usage

insights.claim: Visser calls Google no longer a player in frontier AI for him, arguing Gemini is nowhere near GPT 5.5 or Opus 4.8 and that being the #3 model with open-source models above it is dangerous given recursive self-improvement, but he would never short Google because it is cash-rich and embedded in everyone's life.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Bearish on Google's AI position without endorsing a short; watch for multiple compression like Nvidia/Meta.
insights.quote: Google's not a player in this anymore for me

insights.claim: Visser attributes Google's disadvantage to distraction and scale: ~200,000 employees and a $3 trillion cap supporting Cloud and Workspace, versus Anthropic's under 5,000 employees at roughly a $1.5 trillion private valuation focused purely on enterprise model adoption.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Focus and talent concentration, not just capital, may determine model-race winners.

insights.claim: Visser notes Google lost two senior DeepMind people this week, likely paid around a billion dollars to move to Anthropic, and warns more defections are likely if Gemini doesn't ship a new model soon; he adds GPT 5.5 overtook Google's Nano Banana on image generation.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Talent flight plus loss of its strongest consumer feature are tangible leading indicators of Google's AI decline.

themes.theme: Anthropic valuation and the capex air pocket

insights.claim: The host cites reporting that Anthropic has net dollar retention over 500%, nine of the Fortune 10 as customers, can sign two eight-figure USD contracts in a single meeting, and that its valuation went from a $380 billion Series G to a $965 billion Series H post-money roughly three months later.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Extreme stickiness and a ~3x valuation step in one quarter frame Anthropic as the standout private AI asset.

insights.claim: The host cites reporting that Anthropic turned cash-flow positive with a 15-20% free cash flow margin and 60-70% gross margin, and argues that if true Anthropic looks undervalued and possibly worth $2 trillion given 25-30% month-over-month growth, comparing it only to Tether historically.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: If the cash-flow profile is real, Anthropic is a rare profitable hypergrowth AI asset.

insights.claim: Visser contrasts Anthropic favorably with SpaceX, saying SpaceX's Mars/moon TAM is impossible for him to model whereas Anthropic is something you can actually value, though he remains worried about AI revenue relative to capex.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: A macro manager who avoids SpaceX still sees Anthropic as analyzable, distinguishing the two pre-IPO megacaps.

insights.claim: Visser warns of a 'capex air pocket' over the next three to six months, arguing AI revenue growth may not match the parabolic charts because customers are now focused on reducing token cost, and the Micron news doesn't change the positioning shakeout.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Flags a near-term risk window for AI names even within a structurally bullish view.
insights.quote: there might be I called this a capex air pocket

insights.claim: Visser says he pays $200 a month for his AI tool, is not charged for tokens, and there is no way the provider is profitable on him as a heavy all-day user, illustrating the unresolved unit-economics question for model providers.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Highlights the gap between subscription pricing and compute cost that the revenue-vs-capex debate hinges on.
insights.quote: There's no way they're profitable on me

themes.theme: Agentic loops, jobs, and stablecoin commerce

insights.claim: Visser argues a small number of power users running agentic loops are effectively training their own replacements, meaning AI-driven job losses could come much faster and Anthropic's future revenue must come from companies shedding employees out of a ~$20 trillion annual US compensation pool.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Links AI provider revenue directly to labor displacement; watch employment data as the monetization signal.
insights.quote: these people are training their replacements at this point

insights.claim: Visser says the US is growing GDP rapidly without hiring, with almost no net job creation over the past year (recent gains mostly healthcare), so profits accrue to companies that are not firing but simply not hiring.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: A jobless-growth dynamic supports margins but raises social/political risk and an eventual labor shock.
insights.quote: We are growing rapidly without hiring people

insights.claim: Visser predicts agentic loops will connect to agentic commerce so that more transactions happen via agents than humans by 2030 or sooner, which only stablecoins (not physical money) can settle, citing Stripe sessions where agent-to-agent commerce drives crypto network effects.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Positions stablecoins and crypto rails as the settlement layer for a coming agent-driven commerce wave.
insights.quote: certainly by 2030 but even sooner more transactions will be happening via agents than humans

insights.claim: Visser argues entrepreneurs and soloreneurs have a large advantage in the AI world because, lacking employees, they collaborate with AI daily and run loops to improve their own workflows, citing Stripe's highlighting of this trend.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Implies value accrues to small AI-native operators over large headcount-heavy incumbents.

insights.claim: Visser calls the simultaneous selloff in Bitcoin, gold, and silver a 'debasement capitulation,' arguing the debasement trade (long gold/silver/Bitcoin, short bonds) was overweighted by wealth managers and pensions and is now washing out even though the currency is still being debased.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Frames the metals/Bitcoin drawdown as positioning unwind, not a fundamental shift, implying eventual recovery.

insights.claim: Visser says Kevin Warsh signaling a more hawkish stance triggered the dollar rally and the gold/silver decline, but dismisses it because the US still has a deficit and massive debt that won't change due to rhetoric.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Argues the hawkish catalyst is transient and the structural debasement case for hard assets remains intact.

insights.claim: Visser argues Bitcoin is being unfairly lumped in with bad assets: a bear market alongside software names like Adobe and Salesforce, hit by quarter-end allocation selling, the debasement-trade unwind, institutional dislike, and Michael Saylor/MicroStrategy blow-up shorts, while retail ('the mob') is not involved.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Argues Bitcoin's weakness is contagion and positioning, not a thesis break, with retail absence marking a potential bottom.
insights.quote: Bitcoin is just lumped in with a bunch of bad stuff right now

insights.claim: Visser predicts a third-wave (Elliott Wave) explosive crypto move when AI agents transact at scale, tied to a rising velocity of money: he cites Caitlin Long, ~$700 trillion of global wealth with two-thirds in dormant assets, and tokenization unlocking that wealth to raise GDP and reduce middleman/tax friction.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: Ties the crypto bull case to a macro mechanism (rising money velocity via tokenized assets and agent transactions).

insights.claim: Visser notes Micron went from $60 to about $1,200 over the time he and the host have done the show, using it to underline how violently asset prices can move.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser
insights.so_what: A concrete reminder of the magnitude of repricing possible in AI-exposed names.
insights.quote: Micron was $60 when you and I started this. It's now 1,200 and change
